texts.title: Tea Ceremony
texts.subtitle: Explore the entire ritual that takes place in Japan
texts.text_1: It is the search for unity between people, nature, and objects. It represents balance and the absence of conflict in the tea space.
texts.text_2: The ability to recognize the dignity of everything around us, from guests to utensils, treating each element with deep consideration.
texts.text_3: It refers not only to physical cleansing, but also to spiritual clarity. It is the act of leaving behind the worries of the outside world upon entering the ritual.
texts.text_4: It is the state of profound peace that is achieved when the three previous principles are mastered. It is the imperturbable calm of the spirit.
texts.subtitle_2: DISCOVER THE ART OF THE COMPLETE RITUAL

historia.title: History
historia.subtitle_1: A journey that began more than a thousand years ago
historia.historia-1:
The Japanese tea ceremony, known as Chadō or Sadō (茶道), literally means “The Way of Tea”. More than just a beverage, it represents a philosophy of life based on calm, respect, and mindfulness of the present moment.
Tea arrived in Japan around the 9th century, brought from China by Buddhist monks traveling in search of spiritual knowledge. During their long hours of meditation, tea helped them stay awake and focused. Over time, what began as a simple practice transformed into a refined ritual full of symbolism, aesthetics, and spirituality.
historia.historia_2: During the 12th century, the monk Eisai introduced powdered green tea known as matcha to Japan. This type of tea quickly became a popular beverage among Zen monks. For practitioners of Zen Buddhism, preparing and drinking tea was more than a habit: it was a form of moving meditation. Every gesture—from cleaning the utensils to pouring the tea—had to be performed with mindfulness and tranquility. This spiritual approach began to influence Japanese culture, laying the foundation for what would later become the tea ceremony.
historia.historia_3: During the 14th and 15th centuries, tea ceased to be solely a monastery beverage and began to be practiced at social gatherings among samurai, nobles, and artists. But it was the master Sen no Rikyū (1522–1591) who transformed the tea ritual into a true aesthetic and spiritual philosophy. Rikyū introduced principles that still define the modern tea ceremony: simplicity, naturalness, humility, and harmony with the environment. He also promoted the aesthetics of Wabi-sabi, which celebrates the beauty of the simple, the imperfect, and the ephemeral.
historia.subtitle_4: The four principles of the way of tea
historia.text_1: Every tea ceremony is based on four fundamental principles established by Sen no Rikyū: WA(和) — Harmony The balanced relationship between people, nature, and objects. KEI(敬) — Respect The sincere recognition of the value of each person present. SEI(清) — Purity The physical and spiritual cleanliness of the space and the heart. JAKU(寂) — Tranquility The state of inner peace that arises when the three previous principles are practiced. These principles not only guide the tea ceremony but also a way of living with awareness and serenity.
historia.subtitle_5: The tea room: a space to stop time
historia.text_2: The tea ceremony is traditionally performed in a small room called a Chashitsu. This space is designed to convey calm: simple walls natural materials soft lighting minimal decoration Guests enter through a low door that requires them to bow slightly, reminding them that everyone is equal within the ritual, regardless of their social status. Inside the room, time seems to slow down.
historia.text_3: There is a Japanese concept deeply connected to the tea ceremony: Ichigo Ichie (一期一会) It means: “One meeting, one opportunity.” Every tea ceremony is unique and will never be exactly the same again. Therefore, both the host and the guests should experience the moment with complete attention and gratitude. Drinking tea thus becomes a reminder of something essential: ever-forgotten moments in life are unrepeatable.

ritual.title: The Tea Ritual
ritual.subtitle_1: A moment of calm, gesture by gesture
ritual.text_1: The Japanese tea ceremony is not just about preparing a drink. It is an experience where every movement has meaning.
ritual.text_2: Before entering the tea room, guests perform a symbolic gesture: washing their hands and rinsing their mouths. This act represents leaving behind the worries of the outside world. It is not just about physical cleanliness, but about preparing the mind for a moment of calm and mindfulness. At that moment, the ritual truly begins.
ritual.subtitle_3: Step 2: Entrance to the tea room
ritual.text_3: Guests enter the tea room called Chashitsu, a simple and serene space designed to foster tranquility. The entrance is usually small, requiring a slight bow upon entering. This gesture symbolizes something important: inside the room, everyone is equal. Social status, profession, or wealth don't matter. Only the shared moment matters.
ritual.text_4: The host begins by carefully cleaning each utensil in front of the guests: the bowl (Chawan) the bamboo spoon (Chashaku) the whisk (Chasen) Although the utensils are already clean, this gesture has a profound meaning. It represents respect for the guests and for the ritual itself. Each movement is slow, precise, and full of intention.
ritual.text_5: The host places the matcha powder into the bowl and adds hot water. Then, using the chasen (bamboo whisk), they mix the tea with gentle, rhythmic movements. Gradually, a bright green foam appears on the surface. This moment is one of the most important in the ritual, because it combines technique, concentration, and harmony.
ritual.text_6: Once prepared, the tea bowl is presented to the main guest. The guest receives it with both hands, gently turns the bowl as a sign of respect, and then drinks the tea. After drinking, the bowl is gently wiped and passed to the next guest. This gesture symbolizes trust, gratitude, and connection between those present.
ritual.text_7:
At the end of the ceremony, the silence usually lingers for a few more seconds. There is a Japanese concept that sums up this moment:

Ichigo Ichie

One meeting, one opportunity. It means that each tea ceremony is unique and will never be repeated in the same way. That is why both the host and the guests try to experience the moment with complete attention and gratitude.

utensilios.text_1: Click on a utensil to see its details
utensilios.text_2: Tea bowl. Container where ceremonial matcha is prepared and served.
utensilios.text_3: Bamboo whisk. Used to whisk tea until a perfect froth is obtained.
utensilios.text_4: Bamboo spoon. Used to measure and place the exact amount of matcha in the bowl
utensilios.text_5: Tea container. Usually made of lacquered wood, it holds powdered tea before it is served.
utensilios.text_6: Iron pot. Used to boil water over the brazier during the ceremony
utensilios.text_7: Bamboo ladle. It has a long handle and is used to pour hot water into the Chawan.
utensilios.text_8: Silk cloth. Used by the host for the symbolic purification of the utensils
utensilios.text_9: Disposal container. This is where the water used to rinse the bowl is poured.

saludos.mañana: Good morning! Welcome to the tea path.
saludos.tarde: Good afternoon! Find your moment of calm.
saludos.noche: Good night! A ritual to end the day.
